questions.question: Your best five kilometer (5K) run is 21:42. If a Cessna 172 above cruises at 140 knots (groundspeed), how much sooner would the Cessna 172 finish a five kilometer race?
If it takes five minutes to climb to 7400 ft MSL from sea level and if the same rate of climb is sustained, how long will it take to climb from sea level to 18,000 ft MSL?

A Cessna 210 climbs from an airport (250 ft MSL) to 5000 ft MSL in 4:30. Then, it takes 5:20 to climb from 5000 ft MSL to 10,000 ft MSL. A finally, it takes 6:40 to climb from 10,000 ft MSL to 15,000 MSL. What is the average rate of climb the Cessna 210 attains in the climb from the airport to 15,000 MSL?

You are currently level at 7500 ft MSL cruising towards a national park. This national park has a minimum "over-fly" altitude of 9600 ft MSL. At 20 NM from the nearest boundary of the national park, you plan to climb to cross over this boundary at 11,500 ft MSL. If your TAS is 120 knots, on a TC of 123°, and winds aloft of 180° at 22 knots. What rate of climb is needed to proceed as planned?
With winds aloft of 112° at 34 knots, TAS of 265 km/hr, and a TC of 057°, what is the groundspeed and wind correction angle (WCA)?
With winds aloft of 244° at 29 kts, TAS of 182 mph, and a TC of 111°, what is the groundspeed and wind correction angle (WCA)?

You maintain a TAS of 195 mph and TC of 105°. The first portion of the flight (120 NM) the winds aloft are 221° at 34 kts. The second portion of the flight (100 NM) the winds aloft switch to 091° at 45 kts. In the above flight what is the average groundspeed?
Your aircraft is weighs 24,000 lbs and the CG is at station 120. If 250 kg are removed from station 179, how far forward does the CG move and what is the new CG?

choices.text: The CG moves 1.38 station units aft to station 121.38
The CG moves 1.38 station units forward to station 118.62
The CG moves 0.62 station units aft to station 120.62
The CG moves 0.62 station units forward to station 119.38

questions.correctChoice: The CG moves 1.38 station units forward to station 118.62

questions.question: This time you are flying an aircraft with a weight of 99,500 lbs and a CG at station 210. Several crates of oil, 200 U.S. Gallons of Oil, are moved from station 280 (aft cargo hold) to station 120 (forward cargo hold). What is the new CG?

A turboprop is loaded to a weight of 35,000 lbs. There is a system that allows for credits or reductions of weight for children onboard. The reduction is 100 lbs per child, and there are five children onboard (three at station 200 and two at station 400). What is the new CG if the original CG is at station 360?

One afternoon you are preparing for a lesson in a Cessna 172, and your instructor provides you with this hypothetical situation: At 7500 ft MSL, you lose your engine. The winds aloft are 120° at 25 knots all the way to the surface. Airport #1 (field elevation of 3100 ft MSL) is 10 NM on a true course of 310°. Airport #2 (field elevation of 350 ft MSL) is 13 NM on a true course of 145°. Your glide rate of descent is 700 FPM and your average TAS is 80 knots. Assuming instantaneous turns to the airports. [[NEXT_LINE]] Which, if any, airport can make it to?

choices.text: You can make both but only barely by 0.1 to 0.25 nautical miles.
You come up short to both airports by more than 1 nautical mile.
You come up short to Airport #1 by 1.2 nautical miles but can land at Airport #2.
You can land at Airport #1 but come up short to Airport #2 by 3.2 nautical miles.

questions.correctChoice: You can land at Airport #1 but come up short to Airport #2 by 3.2 nautical miles.

questions.question: You are 22 NM from a VOR. With a groundspeed 200 km/hr, how long does it take to travel clockwise from radial 090° to radial 120°?
If the time between two radials clockwise, 320° and 015°, is 4:37 and the distance to the VOR is 12 NM, what is the groundspeed between the radials?

One evening, you are flying with a groundspeed of 120 knots, an indicated airspeed of 120 knots, OAT is -40°C, and at a pressure altitude of 7500 ft. If the winds aloft are 170° at 20 knots and the wind correction angle is to the right, what is the true course and the true heading?
You are told that you are flying at 0.42M with an OAT of -20°F at FL310 and 29.92" Hg for an altimeter setting in your area. What is the true airspeed and how much slower than 1.00M are you flying?

choices.text: 475 mph true airspeed and 294 km/hr slower than 1.00M
256 mph true airspeed and 346 mph slower than 1.00M
475 km/hr true airspeed and 655 km/hr slower than 1.00M
294 mph true airspeed and 398 km/hr slower than 1.00M

questions.correctChoice: 475 km/hr true airspeed and 655 km/hr slower than 1.00M

questions.question: If the OAT is -22°F, your indicated altitude is 6400 ft, and your true altitude is 6600 ft, what is the pressure altitude (PA) and density altitude (DA)?
Given the calibrated altitude is 17,000 ft, the pressure altitude is 18,000 ft, and the OAT is -05°C, what is the true altitude?
You are cruising at an IAS/CAS of 300 km/hr. The OAT is +20°F. The pressure altitude is 10,000 ft. What is your TAS?

On another afternoon, you are flying a true heading of 270°. You measure your wind drift to be 4° L. On another true heading of 030°, you measure your wind drift to be 6° R. With a TAS of 140 kts, what are the winds aloft?

You have 20 Imp. Gallons onboard and burn fuel at 5.8 US Gal./hr. The winds aloft are 220° at 22 kts. You sustain a 144 KTAS. You are on a true course of 100° out and 280° back. If you need 45 minutes reserve fuel upon returning to your base, how far out can you travel before having to return to base?

Your instructor tells you that you are given a true airspeed of 210 kts, winds aloft of 040° at 32 kts, and on a true course of 196° out. You also have 80 liters of fuel, burn 7.7 US Gallon per hour, and require a 30 minute fuel reserve. How far can you travel outbound before having to return to base?

The altimeter setting in your area is 30.87" Hg. Your current indicated altitude is 7500 ft with an OAT of -20°C. You travel a couple of hundred miles to another area where the new altimeter setting is 29.67" Hg. and an OAT of +20°C. How much does your true altitude change when traveling between the first area to the second area?

You are flying a large aircraft weighing 125,000 kg. A pallet of cargo (1300 lbs) is removed from station 250. If this results in a aft movement of the CG of 2.2 inches, what is the original and new CG?

choices.text: Original CG at station 203.5 and new CG at station 205.7
Original CG at station 465 and new CG at station 467.2
Original CG at station 715 and new CG at station 717.2
Original CG at station 296.5 and new CG at station 298.7

questions.correctChoice: Original CG at station 715 and new CG at station 717.2

questions.question: On a true heading of 024°, you experience a dr ift of 4° L. On a true heading of 311°, you experience a drift of 9° R. With a TAS of 310 km/hr, what are the winds aloft?
Cruising at 0.56M with an OAT of -20°F. The altimeter setting is 29.41" Hg and winds aloft are 210° at 44 kts. With a true course of 311°, what is the true airspeed and groundspeed?

choices.text: True Airspeed = 344 knots and Groundspeed = 350 knots
True Airspeed = 338 knots and Groundspeed = 357 knots
True Airspeed = 355 knots and Groundspeed = 362 knots
True Airspeed = 363 knots and Groundspeed = 371 knots

questions.correctChoice: True Airspeed = 344 knots and Groundspeed = 350 knots

questions.question: Using the above information, your first choice is 34,000 feet. What is the fuel burn, for the entire flight?
questions.context: NIKE is interviewing you for a position flying their Gulfstream V. They are asking you some hypothetical questions to gage your response. Some background information is that you have 13,000 lbs of fuel onboard. The interviewer says for this flight the GV burns fuel at 2000 lbs/hr (above 32,000 feet) and 2200 lbs/hr (below 32,000 feet). Flying at 0.78M with the following winds aloft: [[TABLE]] You are traveling 2120 miles from Hillsboro, OR (HIO) to Honolulu, HI (HNL). The True Course from HIO to HNL is 232°.

questions.question: Using the above information, your next selection is to look at 36,000 feet. What is the time en route and fuel burn, for the entire flight?

choices.text: Time in flight = 306 minutes and Fuel burn = 10250 lbs
Time in flight = 312 minutes and Fuel burn = 10440 lbs
Time in flight = 317 minutes and Fuel burn = 10620 lbs
Time in flight = 323 minutes and Fuel burn = 10790 lbs

questions.correctChoice: Time in flight = 323 minutes and Fuel burn = 10790 lbs
questions.context: NIKE is interviewing you for a position flying their Gulfstream V. They are asking you some hypothetical questions to gage your response. Some background information is that you have 13,000 lbs of fuel onboard. The interviewer says for this flight the GV burns fuel at 2000 lbs/hr (above 32,000 feet) and 2200 lbs/hr (below 32,000 feet). Flying at 0.78M with the following winds aloft: [[TABLE]] You are traveling 2120 miles from Hillsboro, OR (HIO) to Honolulu, HI (HNL). The True Course from HIO to HNL is 232°.

questions.question: Using the above information, you are asked to calculate a time to turn to return to base with a 45 minutes of reserve fuel? Flown at 30,000 feet.
questions.context: NIKE is interviewing you for a position flying their Gulfstream V. They are asking you some hypothetical questions to gage your response. Some background information is that you have 13,000 lbs of fuel onboard. The interviewer says for this flight the GV burns fuel at 2000 lbs/hr (above 32,000 feet) and 2200 lbs/hr (below 32,000 feet). Flying at 0.78M with the following winds aloft: [[TABLE]] You are traveling 2120 miles from Hillsboro, OR (HIO) to Honolulu, HI (HNL). The True Course from HIO to HNL is 232°.

questions.question: You takeoff using the calculations from question 27. Just prior to the halfway point the CEO comes up to the flight deck and asks, "What if we changed altitudes at the halfway point to 30,000 feet from 36,000 feet? Can we get there faster?" If the change is instantaneous, you say:

choices.text: Yes, we would get there 3 minutes faster.
Yes, we would get there 8 minutes faster.
Yes, we would get there 13 minutes faster.
No, we would be there 3 minutes later

questions.correctChoice: Yes, we would get there 13 minutes faster.

[[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 27: Using the above information, your next selection is to look at 36,000 feet. What is the time en route and fuel burn, for the entire flight?

questions.question: You fly for 2.5 hours out over the ocean at 34,000 feet and have to return to Hillsboro at the same altitude. The new winds on the return will be 240° at 21 knots. How much fuel will you have upon return to HIO?
questions.context: NIKE is interviewing you for a position flying their Gulfstream V. They are asking you some hypothetical questions to gage your response. Some background information is that you have 13,000 lbs of fuel onboard. The interviewer says for this flight the GV burns fuel at 2000 lbs/hr (above 32,000 feet) and 2200 lbs/hr (below 32,000 feet). Flying at 0.78M with the following winds aloft: [[TABLE]] You are traveling 2120 miles from Hillsboro, OR (HIO) to Honolulu, HI (HNL). The True Course from HIO to HNL is 232°.

questions.question: 92 pints of oil = _______________________ liters of oil
120 Imp. Gallons of AVGAS = ____________________ kg of AVGAS
Mach 1 at +40°F = ________________________ mph true airspeed
4414 liters of AVGAS = _________________________ lbs of AVGAS
